summary: Matrix Bio is a therapeutic program developing small-molecule hyaluronidase inhibitors to increase high-molecular-weight hyaluronic acid (HMW-HA) in tissues, translating cancer-resistant biology from naked mole rats to humans. The document outlines the project concept, mechanism targeting the HA-CD44 axis for cancer prevention/treatment and longevity applications, team composition (led by Vera Gorbunova, PhD at University of Rochester), and planned formation of a new company with VitaDAO. Development stage and specific experimental data are not provided in this document section.

content_blocks.title: Project Title and Therapeutic Concept
content_blocks.content: Matrix Bio aims to translate cancer-resistant and pro-longevity biology of naked mole rat (NMR) to humans by increasing high-molecular-weight hyaluronic acid (HMW-HA) in tissues. Uses proprietary functional screening assay to discover and optimize small-molecule inhibitors of hyaluronidases (enzymes that degrade hyaluronan). Approach shifts balance toward HMW-HA and away from low-molecular-weight HA (LMW-HA) to suppress tumor progression and improve healthspan.

content_blocks.title: Target Indication and Patient Population
content_blocks.content: Primary indication: Cancer prevention and treatment via modulation of tumor microenvironment. Secondary/potential indications: ECM- and skin-related conditions (wound healing, arthritis, joint and skin conditions) and broader healthspan/longevity indications. Patient population: Cancer caused ~10 million deaths and 19.3 million new cases worldwide in 2020.

content_blocks.title: Mechanism of Action
content_blocks.content: Small-molecule hyaluronidase inhibitors prevent enzymatic degradation of HA, thereby increasing HMW-HA levels and reducing LMW-HA fragments. HMW-HA, as seen in naked mole rat and NMR-HAS2 transgenic mice, supports tissue homeostasis, suppresses tumor initiation and metastasis via CD44-mediated signaling. Associated with improved healthspan and ~10% lifespan extension in mice.

content_blocks.title: Biological Target
content_blocks.content: Primary targets: Hyaluronidase enzymes responsible for HA breakdown (two prioritized targets, one undisclosed for IP reasons). Indirect targets: HA-CD44 axis and extracellular matrix composition.

content_blocks.title: Current Development Stage
content_blocks.content: Development stage field is present in document template but not filled in. No specific experimental progress data provided (e.g., computational modeling, in vitro validation, in vivo studies, candidate selection status).

content_blocks.title: Development Timeline and Milestones
content_blocks.content: Template section for next 3 major milestones with estimated dates is present but not populated with specific information.

content_blocks.title: Team Information
content_blocks.content: Key Personnel: Vera Gorbunova, PhD (Principal Investigator/Scientific Founder); VitaDAO/Longevity Dealflow Team for scientific/business evaluation and company formation support (Sebastian Brunemeier, Tim Peterson, Rhys Anderson, Koen De Lombaert, etc.). Affiliations: University of Rochester, Rochester Aging Research Center (Gorbunova Lab); Matrix Bio newco to be launched jointly by VitaDAO and Gorbunova lab. Expertise: Comparative biology of aging and longevity, tumor suppression mechanisms, genome stability, extracellular matrix and hyaluronan biology, cancer biology, translational aging research, biotech/DeSci company formation and IP strategy.

key_findings: Therapeutic approach based on translating naked mole rat biology: small-molecule hyaluronidase inhibitors to increase HMW-HA
Proprietary functional screening assay mentioned for discovering inhibitors
Mechanism involves CD44-mediated signaling pathway for tumor suppression
Published evidence cited: NMR-HAS2 transgenic mice show ~10% lifespan extension and improved healthspan
Two prioritized hyaluronidase targets (one undisclosed for IP reasons)
Large addressable market: 19.3 million cancer cases annually worldwide
Strong academic team led by Vera Gorbunova at University of Rochester with relevant expertise
New company formation planned with VitaDAO partnership
Development stage, specific experimental data, milestones, and IP status not disclosed in this document section
